title: AWS Distinguished Eng: Learnings From 3000 Incidents And How Engineering Is Changing | Marc Brooker
guest_role: Distinguished Engineer at AWS (databases/serverless), new grad to DE at Amazon

sections.title: How AI will change software engineering
Advice for junior engineers given AI

key_takeaways: Finding problems that matter means going super broad: intersect customer pain, bottom-up hardware trends, and macro industry shifts — Aurora Serverless and DSQL both came from colliding a customer signal (serverless devs stuck with serverful databases) with a technical trend (object storage as durability layer).

He stayed on call for 15 years because most of his practical distributed-systems knowledge came from on-call and postmortems; rote ticket-closing should be automated so deep experts spend on-call time on genuinely unexpected behavior.

AWS's weekly company-wide COE/postmortem review meeting is, in his view, 'a core, almost causal factor behind AWS's success' because it forces top engineers and leaders to deeply understand how systems actually operate.

A great postmortem digs through multiple levels of why (code bug, testing gap, organizational cause) with concrete fixes at each level, and cross-postmortem patterns should become services or whole-class fixes — DSQL was designed against common relational outage patterns using MVCC plus optimistic commit checks so readers and writers never block each other (MVCC storage overhead typically under ~10%).

Bad postmortem culture has two failure modes: insufficient focus on outcomes, and normalization of operational heroics — heroic on-calls feel like strong ownership from the inside but are an expensive break-fix cycle.

Caches create dangerous modality: a warm-cache fast mode and a cold-cache mode where the un-scaled backend collapses into a metastable failure that is stable while down; rare per-system yet an underlying cause of a majority of the industry's biggest outages — prefer complete materialized views, local copies of slow-moving data, or scalable backends.

Software has been supply-constrained for its ~60-year history and remains so; the mainstream will move to agentic/AI-powered/specification-driven development, while hand-coding persists as craft (like hand joinery) and legacy niches stay economically real but shrinking.

For junior engineers: customer, business, and economic context is moving to the earliest career stages; the pure 8-hours-in-the-IDE career is shrinking, but deep technical or scientific expertise is more leveraged than ever because asking the right questions matters more.

For senior engineers: 'if you aren't doing it hands on, your opinion about it is very likely to be completely wrong' — influence-role leaders must get back to hands-on building with agentic tools, which takes humility for people with fancy titles.

Writing scales expertise across space and time and forces mental clarity; he writes docs he never shares just to sharpen thinking, and documenting which design decisions were carefully reasoned versus arbitrary is almost as important as the decisions themselves.
From his doing-vs-discussing 2x2: 100% at either pole is a failure mode; his sweet spot is ~75/25 practitioner-to-communicator, and long-term it is better to be underrated than overrated.

Advice to his younger self: be bolder about changing teams — he changed organizations 4 times and 5-6 would have been optimal; keep asking what you're learning and who you're learning from, and follow curiosity.

notable_quotes.speaker: Marc Brooker
notable_quotes.quote: The majority of my in practice knowledge about how to build distributed systems has come from being on call and analyzing and deeply understanding these post mortems and COEs.

notable_quotes.speaker: Marc Brooker
notable_quotes.quote: We are in this minute that if you aren't doing it hands on, your opinion about it is very likely to be completely wrong. And that takes a level of humility to admit.

notable_quotes.speaker: Marc Brooker
notable_quotes.quote: Writing lets you scale out the impact of your expertise in space and time in a way that's really hard to do in other media.
